whatItDoes: Interactive regular expression reference covering character classes, quantifiers, anchors, groups, lookaheads, and flags with real-world examples. Each pattern includes a description, example input/output, and common use cases.
whoShouldUse: Developers writing or debugging regex patterns, data analysts building data extraction pipelines, QA engineers validating input formats, and students learning regular expressions.
whyItExists: Regular expressions are powerful but notoriously difficult to memorize. This interactive cheatsheet provides instant access to every regex concept with practical examples, eliminating the need to search external documentation.
useCases: Look up the exact syntax for a positive lookahead assertion
Find the correct character class for matching Unicode letters
Reference common email and URL validation patterns
Understand the difference between greedy and lazy quantifiers with examples
Quickly find how to match whitespace or word boundaries
instructions: Browse regex patterns by category: Character Classes, Quantifiers, Anchors, Groups, Lookarounds, Flags
Click on any pattern to expand its description and examples
Use the copy button to copy the regex snippet to your clipboard
Read the example input/output to understand how each pattern behaves
Combine patterns by referencing multiple sections for complex regex construction
examples: Category: Character Classes. Pattern: \d matches digits, \w matches word characters, \s matches whitespace. Example: \d{3}-\d{4} matches '555-1234'.
Category: Lookarounds. Pattern: (?=...) positive lookahead. Example: \w+(?=@) matches 'user' in 'user@email.com' without including the @ symbol.
bestPractices: Start with simple patterns and add complexity incrementally — test each addition
Use non-capturing groups (?:...) when you don't need to extract the matched group
Prefer specific character classes over . (dot) to avoid unintended matches
Use the regex tester tool alongside this cheatsheet to validate patterns in real-time
commonMistakes: Using . (dot) to match newlines — it doesn't by default; use [\s\S] or the 's' flag
Forgetting that ? after a quantifier makes it lazy, not optional
Confusing ^ inside [] (negation) with ^ outside [] (start of string anchor)
Writing regex for email validation that is too strict or too permissive
faq: What's the difference between * and +? * matches zero or more (can match nothing), + matches one or more (must match at least once).
When should I use the 'g' (global) flag? Use it when you want to find all matches in a string, not just the first one.
How do I match literal special characters? Escape them with a backslash: \. matches a literal dot.
Can regex match HTML? Technically yes, but it's fragile and not recommended. Use a proper HTML parser instead.
